Chapter 3 — Prompting Claude Like an Ins — Marta's problem is not that Claude is bad. Her problem is that her prompts describe a topic rather than a teaching situa

S02.meta: // SLIDE 02 — WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

data.title: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO.
blocks: · Translate a teaching goal into a structured Claude brief.
· Identify the eight fields that separate a useful educational prompt from a generic one.
· Recognize common prompt failures and their instructional consequences.
· Use reusable prompt patterns for lessons, feedback, adaptations, tutoring sessions, and rubric audits.
data.section: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

S03.meta: // SLIDE 03 — CORE CONCEPT — PROMPTING AS IN

data.title: CORE CONCEPT — PROMPTING AS INSTRUCTIONAL SPECIFICATION
blocks: Robert Gagne
M. David Merrill
Grant Wiggins and Jay McTighe

data.title: THE EIGHT-FIELD INSTRUCTIONAL BRIEF.
blocks: The following eight fields make up a reusable instructional brief. Each field prevents a specific failure.
The Eight-Field Instructional Brief — Who are these students? Grade level, course, rough context, language situation, any relevant access needs.

data.title: A WORKED TEACHING WALKTHROUGH.
blocks: · Is the learner accurately described in the output?
· Is the prior knowledge assumption correct?
· Does the activity actually surface and address the misconception?
· Does the activity lead toward the stated outcome?
· Are the constraints respected?

S07.meta: // SLIDE 07 — THE HUMAN GATE — WHAT STAYS WI

data.title: THE HUMAN GATE — WHAT STAYS WITH YOU
blocks: · Outcome validity. You decide what learning matters for your students in your course context. Claude can suggest; it cann
· Misconception accuracy. You know what your students actually believe. The misconception field is only as good as your pe
· Classroom fit. Claude does not know that your third-period class moves faster than your first, that one student has a 50
· Example authenticity. Claude generates plausible examples. Plausible is not the same as true, representative, or appropr
· Tone and relationship. Claude does not know your students. Feedback that is technically accurate can still be damaging i
data.section: THE HUMAN GATE — WHAT STAYS WI

data.title: COMMON MISTAKES
blocks: Mistake 1: Describing a topic instead of a situation.
Mistake 2: Omitting the misconception.
Mistake 3: Accepting the first output.
Mistake 4: Forgetting to state what Claude must not do.
Mistake 5: Writing the brief once and reusing it unchanged.
